name: Teochew Steamed Pomfret
description: A clean, comforting steam that lets the silver pomfret's delicate sweetness lead, with salted vegetable, tomato, sour plum and tofu for a gentle savoury-sour broth.

ingredients.name: Whole silver pomfret, cleaned and scored
Teochew salted vegetable (kiam chai), rinsed and sliced

steps: Pat the fish dry and lay it on a heatproof plate deep enough to hold the juices.
Scatter the salted vegetable, ginger, mushrooms, tomato, tofu and sour plums around and over the fish. Lightly crush the plums.
Drizzle over the soy sauce and wine.
Steam over rapidly boiling water for 8–10 minutes, until the flesh at the thickest part turns opaque and pulls from the bone.
Spoon the tangy broth over the fish and serve immediately with rice.
tip: Don't oversteam — pomfret flesh dries out fast. Pull it the moment the centre turns opaque.
inspiredBy: A Teochew home-cooking classic; similar to versions by The Burning Kitchen and Spice N Pans.

name: Assam (Tamarind) Fish Curry
description: A sour-spicy tamarind curry built on a fragrant rempah, perfect for firm red fish, batang steaks or a meaty fish head. Tangy, rich and made for rice.

ingredients.name: Firm fish steaks or fish head
Tamarind pulp, soaked in 400 ml water and strained

steps: Blend the dried chillies, shallots, garlic, lemongrass and belacan into a smooth rempah.
Fry the rempah in oil over medium-low heat until the oil splits and it smells fragrant, 8–10 minutes.
Pour in the strained tamarind water and bring to a simmer. Season with sugar and salt to balance the sourness.
Add the okra and tomatoes, simmer 3 minutes, then slide in the fish.
Simmer gently until the fish is just cooked through, 6–8 minutes, basting rather than stirring so it doesn't break up. Rest 10 minutes before serving — the gravy deepens.
tip: Use a firm fish like batang, red snapper or grouper so the pieces hold together in the gravy.
inspiredBy: A Nonya assam pedas style curry, in the spirit of The Meatmen and local home recipes.

name: BBQ Sambal Stingray
description: Smoky grilled stingray wing smothered in a thick, fiery sambal and finished with calamansi and chinchalok. The quintessential East Coast hawker supper.

ingredients.name: Sambal (dried chilli, shallots, garlic, belacan, blended and fried)

steps: Rinse the stingray wing and pat dry. Lay it on a softened banana leaf.
Spread a thick, even layer of sambal over both sides of the wing.
Grill over charcoal or in a hot oven/griddle, leaf and all, about 6–8 minutes a side, until the flesh flakes into strands along the cartilage.
Serve straight off the leaf with a squeeze of calamansi, raw shallots and chinchalok on the side.
tip: The flesh is done when it peels cleanly off the cartilage in soft fibres — don't wait for browning.
inspiredBy: The classic hawker-stall preparation; close to versions shared by The Meatmen.

name: Sliced Fish Soup
description: A light, milky-sweet broth with tender slices of batang, tomato, lettuce and a hit of ginger — a hawker-centre comfort bowl you can make cleaner at home.

ingredients.name: Light soy, white pepper, sesame oil

steps: Toss the fish slices with a little cornflour, white pepper and sesame oil; set aside.
Bring the stock to a simmer with the ginger and tomato. Season with light soy.
Add the greens and let them wilt, then turn the heat to low.
Slip in the fish slices and poach gently for 1–2 minutes, just until opaque — do not boil hard or they'll toughen.
Stir in evaporated milk if using, and finish with white pepper. Serve over rice or thick bee hoon.
tip: Slice the fish across the grain and poach off a gentle simmer so the pieces stay silky.
inspiredBy: A home version of the hawker 'sliced fish soup', similar to Spice N Pans.

name: Cantonese Steamed Threadfin with Ginger & Scallion
description: The purest way to honour a premium fish: steamed threadfin dressed in hot oil, ginger, spring onion and a sweet-savoury soy. Restaurant results in 20 minutes.
steps: Lay the sliced ginger and white parts of the spring onion on a plate and rest the fish on top so steam circulates underneath.
Steam over high heat 7–9 minutes, until just opaque. Pour away the watery liquid that collects.
Top the fish with the julienned ginger and green spring onion.
Warm the soy, water and sugar together and pour around the fish.
Heat the oil until smoking and pour it over the aromatics so they sizzle. Garnish with coriander.
tip: Pour off the steaming liquid before saucing — it's the secret to a clean-tasting finish.

name: Singapore Chilli Crab
description: The national dish: meaty mud crab in a sweet, savoury, gently spicy tomato-chilli gravy thickened with egg, made for mopping up with fried mantou.

ingredients.name: Live mud crab, cleaned and chopped into sections
Fresh red chilli + dried chilli paste

steps: Lightly fry or steam the crab pieces until the shells just turn orange; set aside.
Fry the garlic, ginger and chilli paste in oil until fragrant and the oil reddens.
Stir in the ketchup, chilli sauce, sugar and soy, then the water. Bring to a simmer and taste — it should be sweet, savoury and tangy.
Return the crab, cover and simmer 5–6 minutes so the meat cooks through and soaks up the gravy.
Thicken with a little cornflour slurry, then turn off the heat and stream in the beaten egg, stirring to make silky ribbons. Serve with mantou.
tip: Crack the claws before saucing so the gravy gets inside, and add the egg off the heat for soft ribbons.
inspiredBy: The Singapore hawker icon; in the spirit of The Meatmen and Spice N Pans.

name: Black Pepper Crab
description: The drier, punchier cousin of chilli crab — crab sections wok-tossed in a buttery, intensely peppery sauce that clings to every shell.
steps: Toast the cracked pepper in a dry wok until aromatic, then set aside.
Lightly fry the crab pieces until the shells turn orange; remove.
Melt the butter, fry the garlic, ginger, curry leaves and chilli until fragrant.
Add the toasted pepper, oyster sauce, dark soy and sugar; stir into a glossy paste.
Return the crab and toss over high heat until well coated and cooked through, 4–5 minutes. Serve at once.
tip: Crack the pepper yourself and toast it — pre-ground pepper just can't deliver the same heat and perfume.
inspiredBy: A zi char and seafood-restaurant staple.

name: Butter Cereal Prawns
description: Crisp prawns tumbled in a buttery, curry-leaf-scented cereal crumble with a whisper of chilli — sweet, savoury and impossible to stop eating.
steps: Pat the prawns very dry and shallow-fry until crisp and bright orange; drain.
In a clean wok, melt the butter and fry the curry leaves and chilli until fragrant and the leaves crisp.
Add the cereal with a pinch of sugar, salt and milk powder; toast gently until golden and sandy.
Return the prawns and toss until every piece is coated in the crunchy cereal. Serve immediately while crisp.
tip: Keep the shells on — they crisp up and carry flavour, and they protect the sweet meat from drying out.
inspiredBy: A zi char favourite, similar to The Meatmen's cereal prawns.

name: Salted Egg Yolk Prawns
description: Golden, grainy salted-egg sauce foaming with butter and curry leaves, clinging to crisp shell-on prawns. Rich, creamy and addictive.

ingredients.name: Salted egg yolks, steamed and mashed

steps: Dust the prawns lightly with cornflour and fry until crisp; drain.
Melt the butter and fry the curry leaves and chilli until fragrant.
Add the mashed salted egg yolks and stir until they foam up and smell nutty.
Splash in the evaporated milk with a little sugar to loosen into a creamy sauce.
Toss the prawns through until fully coated. Serve straight away.
tip: Let the yolks foam in the butter before adding liquid — that foaming stage is where the flavour develops.
inspiredBy: The ubiquitous zi char salted egg treatment.

name: Sambal Sotong
description: Springy squid or cuttlefish in a glossy, sweet-spicy sambal slicked with tamarind and onions — a rice-disappearing dish in the best way.

ingredients.name: Squid or cuttlefish, cleaned and scored

steps: Blend the chillies, shallots, garlic and belacan into a rempah.
Fry the rempah in oil over medium heat until the oil splits and it darkens, 8–10 minutes.
Stir in the tamarind paste and sugar; taste for a sweet-sour-spicy balance.
Add the sliced onion and cook until soft, then turn the heat to high.
Toss in the squid and stir-fry just 2–3 minutes until it curls and turns opaque. Don't overcook or it goes rubbery.
tip: Score the squid in a crosshatch and cook it fast over high heat so it curls prettily and stays tender.

name: Orh Luak (Oyster Omelette)
description: Crispy-edged, gooey-centred sweet potato starch omelette studded with plump oysters, finished with garlic chilli sauce. Pure hawker nostalgia.
steps: Whisk the sweet potato starch with the water into a thin batter.
Heat plenty of oil in a wok, fry the garlic, then pour in the starch batter and let it set and crisp at the edges.
Crack the eggs over the top, break the yolks and let them set into the crispy pancake.
Push everything aside, add the oysters and a splash of fish sauce, then fold together so the oysters stay just-cooked and juicy.
Plate with the crispy bits up, garnish, and serve with garlic-chilli sauce.
tip: Add the oysters near the very end — they only need a minute, and overcooking shrinks and toughens them.
inspiredBy: The hawker oyster omelette, Teochew/Hokkien style.

name: Stir-fried Cockles with Kang Kong
description: Just-blanched blood cockles tossed with water spinach in a punchy sambal-belacan — quick, briny and deeply satisfying.
steps: Blanch the cockles in just-boiled water for 10–20 seconds, then shell them, keeping any juice.
Get a wok smoking hot. Fry the garlic, sambal and taucheo until fragrant.
Add the kang kong stems first, then the leaves, and toss over high heat until just wilted.
Add the cockles and their juice with a pinch of sugar, toss for 20–30 seconds to warm through, and serve immediately.
tip: Cockles cook in seconds — they go in last, just to warm through, or they turn rubbery and dry.
inspiredBy: A hawker-style sambal kang kong with cockles.

name: Garlic & Wine Steamed Lala
description: Clams (or mussels and razor clams) steamed open in a fragrant broth of garlic, ginger, Chinese wine and glass noodles that soak up all the sweetness.

ingredients.name: Lala clams (or mussels / razor clams), purged

steps: Soak the clams in salted water for an hour to purge sand, then drain.
Lay the soaked glass noodles in a heatproof bowl. Top with garlic, ginger and the clams.
Pour over the stock, wine and soy.
Steam over high heat 6–8 minutes, until the shells open and the noodles are slippery and flavour-soaked.
Discard any unopened shells, scatter with chilli and spring onion, and serve in the bowl.
tip: Discard any shellfish that won't open after steaming — and any that were already open and won't close before cooking.

name: Sweet & Sour Crispy Tilapia
description: Whole tilapia fried until shatteringly crisp, then bathed in a glossy sweet-sour sauce with pineapple and peppers. A kid-friendly family favourite.
steps: Score the fish to the bone, pat dry and dust all over (and into the cuts) with cornflour.
Deep-fry in hot oil until golden and crisp, 6–8 minutes, then drain on a rack.
In a wok, fry the onion and capsicum briefly, then add ketchup, vinegar, sugar and a splash of water.
Add the pineapple, bring to a simmer and thicken with the slurry into a glossy sauce.
Pour the hot sauce over the crispy fish just before serving so it stays crunchy.
tip: Sauce the fish at the table or right before eating — pour too early and the lovely crust goes soggy.
inspiredBy: A classic Cantonese-style sweet and sour whole fish.

name: Steamed Grouper in Superior Soy
description: Banquet-style steamed grouper (or snapper, seabass) with hot oil, ginger, spring onion and a sweet superior soy — the gold standard for a sweet, gelatinous fish.

ingredients.name: Superior light soy (or light soy + sugar)

steps: Rest the fish on sliced ginger and spring onion whites on a plate so steam flows underneath.
Steam over high heat 8–10 minutes (longer for a whole fish), until the flesh just flakes at the bone.
Tip away the steaming liquid. Crown the fish with julienned ginger and green spring onion.
Warm the soy with the water and a touch of sugar; pour around the fish.
Pour smoking-hot oil over the aromatics to release their fragrance. Garnish and serve.
tip: A whole grouper's head and skin are gelatinous and prized — steam just to set, never past flaky.
inspiredBy: The classic Cantonese steamed fish, restaurant style.

name: Crispy Fried Fish with Sambal
description: Small whole fish — ikan kuning, kembong or milkfish — marinated in turmeric and fried shatteringly crisp, served with a sharp sambal. Bones and all.

ingredients.name: Small whole fish, scaled and gutted
Sambal belacan or sambal chilli, to serve

steps: Pat the fish very dry, then rub inside and out with turmeric and salt. Rest 15 minutes.
Heat the oil until shimmering hot — a small piece of fish should sizzle immediately.
Fry the fish in batches without crowding, 4–6 minutes a side, until deeply golden and crisp.
Drain on a rack (not paper, so they stay crisp). Serve with sambal, calamansi and cucumber.
tip: Dry fish and hot oil are everything — moisture makes the oil spit and the skin go soft instead of crisp.
inspiredBy: The nasi-lemak-style fried fish found at Malay stalls everywhere.

name: Braised Abalone & Sea Cucumber
description: A Chinese New Year treasure dish: tender abalone and silky sea cucumber braised with mushrooms in a glossy superior-stock gravy. Prized for texture and prosperity.
steps: Cut the softened sea cucumber into thick pieces. Blanch with ginger and wine to remove any odour, then drain.
Fry the ginger and spring onion in oil, add the mushrooms, then pour in the stock, oyster sauce, dark soy and wine.
Add the sea cucumber and simmer gently, covered, for 45–60 minutes until silky and the gravy reduces.
Add the abalone in the last 10 minutes (fresh) or just to warm through (canned) — long cooking toughens it.
Thicken the gravy with a little slurry until glossy, and serve, often over blanched greens.
tip: Buy sea cucumber pre-soaked from the dried-goods stall — rehydrating it from scratch takes 2–3 days of daily water changes.
inspiredBy: A classic Cantonese banquet / Chinese New Year braise.

name: Boiled Gong Gong with Chilli Dip
description: The ultimate beer-time nibble: dog conch simply boiled with aromatics, the sweet meat twisted out with a toothpick and dunked in a tangy chilli sauce.

ingredients.name: Red chilli, garlic, calamansi, sugar (blended dip)

steps: Soak the gong gong in salted water for 30 minutes so they purge sand, then scrub the shells.
Bring a pot of water with ginger, lemongrass and salt to a rolling boil.
Tip in the gong gong and boil 4–6 minutes, until the meat just sets — don't overboil or it turns rubbery.
Meanwhile blend the chilli, garlic, sugar and calamansi into a tangy dipping sauce.
Serve hot in a bowl with toothpicks — twist the meat out of each shell and dip.
tip: Twist, don't yank — turning the toothpick as you pull keeps the curl of meat whole instead of snapping off the tip.
inspiredBy: The classic zi char / seafood-stall gong gong.
